Δείγμα 1

Λοβόλιθος - Σπασμένος. Διακρίνεται το σχήμα της εσωτερικής κοιλότητας.

en.scyphocrinites_elegans-περιγραφή-1: Scyphocrinites elegans is an extinct camerate crinoid that lived during the latest Silurian to earliest Devonian, approximately 430–415 million years ago. Its fossils are widely distributed across Europe (including the type locality in Bohemia), North Africa (notably Morocco), North America, and parts of Asia, often occurring in dense fossil horizons near the Silurian-Devonian boundary.
en.scyphocrinites_elegans-περιγραφή-2: What sets Scyphocrinites elegans apart from most other stalked crinoids is its remarkable adaptation for a semi-pelagic lifestyle. Instead of a conventional holdfast anchoring it to the seafloor, it possessed a large, bulbous, chambered structure at the distal end of its long stem known as a lobolith (historically described as a separate genus, Camarocrinus). This lobolith is widely interpreted as a buoyancy device, likely filled with gas, allowing the animal to float or drift with its crown suspended downward in the water column rather than remaining fixed to the bottom.
en.scyphocrinites_elegans-περιγραφή-3: The species features an elongate-obconical calyx up to about 10 cm long, with distinctive sculptured plates bearing radiating ridges or costae that form intricate patterns. A slender stem could reach lengths of up to 3 meters, ending in the lobolith (up to 20 cm in diameter), while the branching arms extended further for filter feeding in the upper water levels.
en.scyphocrinites_elegans-περιγραφή-4: Fossils of Scyphocrinites elegans, including associated loboliths, are frequently preserved in three-dimensional detail, especially in Moroccan deposits, highlighting its unique ecological experimentation among Paleozoic crinoids.
